whatItDoes: Interactive Git command reference organized by category (Branching, Stashing, Undoing, History, etc.) with copy-to-clipboard support. Covers essential daily Git workflows from basic commits to advanced rebasing and cherry-picking.
whoShouldUse: Git beginners learning version control, intermediate users expanding their Git knowledge, and experienced developers who need a quick command reference without leaving the terminal context.
whyItExists: Git has dozens of commands with subtle variations. Even experienced developers forget the exact syntax for rebasing, stashing with messages, or undoing specific commits. This cheatsheet puts the most-used commands at your fingertips.
useCases: Quickly find the correct git command to undo the last commit without losing changes
Copy the exact syntax for interactive rebase to squash commits before a pull request
Look up how to create a new branch and switch to it in one command
Find the git command to stash changes with a descriptive message
Reference cherry-pick syntax when applying a specific commit to another branch
instructions: Browse commands by category using the sidebar or tabs
Use the search box to filter commands by keyword
Click the copy icon next to any command to copy it to your clipboard
Read the brief description below each command to understand when to use it
Combine commands by copying multiple commands in sequence for complex workflows
examples: Search: 'squash'. Output: git rebase -i HEAD~N with instructions for marking commits as 'squash' in the editor.
Category: Undoing. Output: Commands for git reset --soft/--mixed/--hard, git revert, git restore, and git stash.
bestPractices: Always create a backup branch before destructive operations like reset --hard or rebase
Use git stash pop instead of git stash apply to remove the stash after applying
Write descriptive commit messages that explain why, not what, the change does
Use git log --oneline --graph to visualize branch structure before rebasing
commonMistakes: Using git reset --hard on committed work without creating a backup branch first
Confusing git merge with git rebase — rebase rewrites history, merge preserves it
Running git push --force on shared branches, overwriting teammates' work
Forgetting to git pull before pushing, causing unnecessary merge conflicts
faq: Is this cheatsheet for Git beginners or advanced users? It covers both — basic commands like add/commit and advanced operations like rebase and cherry-pick.
Are the commands safe to copy-paste directly? Most are safe, but destructive commands (reset --hard, force push) should be reviewed before running.
Can I contribute additional commands? The cheatsheet is static, but you can request additions through the feedback page.
Does this cover Git LFS or Git submodules? Currently it covers core Git commands. LFS and submodule commands may be added later.
